guide_description: Three days covering Rome's essential triad: the baroque historic core, the ancient imperial center, and the Vatican. Each day is geographically self-contained - pick the order based on weather, jet lag, or where you're staying.

days.cluster_tagline: Baroque squares and ancient temples in a walkable historic core
days.neighborhood_description: The dense historic quarter between the Tiber bend and Via del Corso. Roman temples, baroque piazzas, and market squares stacked on top of two millennia of street grid.

morning.notes: Arrive at opening (9am) to see the oculus beam land cleanly on the interior before the floor fills with tour groups. A small entry fee now applies on weekdays; Sunday mass is free but limits movement inside.

afternoon.activity_title: Piazza Navona and Sant'Agnese in Agone
afternoon.notes: Built on the footprint of the Stadium of Domitian, which is why the shape is elongated. The Fountain of the Four Rivers is Bernini; the church facade opposite is by his rival Borromini - the two have been arguing across the piazza since the 1650s.

evening.notes: Lighting comes on around sunset and the fountain is visibly cleaner than the daytime selfie scrum suggests. Approach from Via della Stamperia rather than the main funnel street for the reveal Fellini used in La Dolce Vita.

if_time_permits.notes: Morning produce market until about 2pm, then resets as an aperitivo square by early evening. The brooding statue in the center is Giordano Bruno, burned here for heresy in 1600.

days.cluster_tagline: The imperial heart - Colosseum, Forum, Palatine in one continuous archaeological park
days.neighborhood_description: The southeastern crook of central Rome where the ancient city was governed and entertained. The Colosseum, Forum, and Palatine share a single ticket and a single archaeological zone covering nearly a square kilometer.

morning.notes: Book the timed-entry ticket online days in advance - walk-ups routinely face 2-hour queues. The combined Colosseum/Forum/Palatine ticket is valid for 24 hours so you don't have to do everything today, but most people do. The arena floor access is a separate ticket and worth it if you can get one.

afternoon.notes: Enter from Via dei Fori Imperiali, not the Colosseum side - the elevation walking down through the arches is the cinematic approach. Bring water; there's almost no shade and the marble paths reflect heat. The Forum and Palatine are technically one site but the Palatine climb adds another hour.

evening.activity_title: Piazza del Campidoglio and Capitoline views
evening.notes: Michelangelo's piazza on the Capitoline Hill - climb the cordonata ramp rather than the steep stairs to its right. Walk around the back of the Palazzo Senatorio for one of the best free views of the Forum, especially at golden hour.

if_time_permits.notes: Included with your Forum ticket. The imperial palace ruins are less informational than the Forum but the views down onto the Circus Maximus from the south side are the payoff.

The world's oldest public museum (founded 1471). The original bronze Marcus Aurelius equestrian statue lives here - the one in the piazza outside is a replica. Good rainy-day swap for the Forum if weather collapses.

days.cluster_tagline: The world's smallest country and the largest church in Christendom
days.neighborhood_description: An independent state inside Rome, reached by walking west across the Tiber. The Vatican Museums, Sistine Chapel, and St. Peter's together form one of the most visited religious and cultural sites on earth.

morning.notes: Book online at the official museum site weeks ahead - walk-up lines routinely exceed 3 hours in season. The standard route funnels everyone toward the Sistine Chapel at the end; once inside the chapel you can exit directly to St. Peter's if you go right (most tour groups know this trick, some museum staff will tell you it's only for groups - it's not).

afternoon.notes: Entry is free but the security line in St. Peter's Square can be long. Dress code is enforced - shoulders and knees covered, no exceptions. The dome climb is a separate ticket worth buying at the entrance; elevator gets you most of the way up, then a tight spiral staircase to the lantern.

evening.activity_title: Castel Sant'Angelo and the bridge at sunset
evening.notes: Hadrian's mausoleum turned papal fortress turned museum. Even if you skip the interior, the Ponte Sant'Angelo with its ten Bernini-designed angels lining the bridge is one of the best sunset walks in Rome, with the dome of St. Peter's at your back and the river bending below.

if_time_permits.notes: The Mussolini-era boulevard cut through the medieval Borgo district to give a monumental approach to St. Peter's. Architecturally controversial (it destroyed a charming spina of buildings) but the dome reveal walking west is genuinely cinematic.
